coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical monitoring of bisphenol-s through nanostructured tin oxide/Nafion/GCE: A solution to environmental pollution

coredata.dc:description: Over the past few decades, phenolic compounds have been broadly exploited in the industries to be utilized in several applications including polycarbonate plastic, food containers, epoxy resins, etc. One of the major compounds in phenolics is Bisphenol-S (BPS) which has dominantly replaced Bisphenol-A in several applications. Phenolic compounds are extensively drained into the environment without proper treatment and cause several health hazards. Thus, to tackle this serious problem an electrochemical sensor based on SnO2/GCE has been successfully engineered to monitor the low-level concentration of BPS in water samples. The fabrication of SnO2 nanoparticles (SnO2 NPs) was confirmed through FTIR, XRD, and TEM to examine the size, crystallinity, internal texture, and functionalities of the prepared material. The fabricated material was exploited as a chemically modified sensor for the determination of BPS in water samples collected from different sources. Under optimal conditions such as scan sweep 100 mV/s, PBS electrolyte pH of 6, potential window (0.3–1.3 V), the proposed sensor manifested an excellent response for BPS. The LOD of the present method for BPS was calculated as 0.007 μM, respectively. Moreover, the stability and selectivity profile of SnO2/GCE for BPS in the real matrix was examined to be outstanding.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical monitoring of bisphenol-s through nanostructured tin oxide/Nafion/GCE: A solution to environmental pollution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over the past few decades, phenolic compounds have been broadly exploited in the industries to be utilized in several applications including polycarbonate plastic, food containers, epoxy resins, etc. One of the major compounds in phenolics is Bisphenol-S (BPS) which has dominantly replaced Bisphenol-A in several applications. Phenolic compounds are extensively drained into the environment without proper treatment and cause several health hazards. Thus, to tackle this serious problem an electrochemical sensor based on SnO2/GCE has been successfully engineered to monitor the low-level concentration of BPS in water samples. The fabrication of SnO2 nanoparticles (SnO2 NPs) was confirmed through FTIR, XRD, and TEM to examine the size, crystallinity, internal texture, and functionalities of the prepared material. The fabricated material was exploited as a chemically modified sensor for the determination of BPS in water samples collected from different sources. Under optimal conditions such as scan sweep 100 mV/s, PBS electrolyte pH of 6, potential window (0.3–1.3 V), the proposed sensor manifested an excellent response for BPS. The LOD of the present method for BPS was calculated as 0.007 μM, respectively. Moreover, the stability and selectivity profile of SnO2/GCE for BPS in the real matrix was examined to be outstanding.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, many researchers have worked on food products and their various properties and the study of quality and measurement of its contents (Mehmandoust et al., 2021a, 2021c; Buledi et al., 2022; Karaman et al., 2022; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022a, 2022b). In the past few years, bisphenol S [4, 4′– sulfonyldiphenol] (BPS) has been widely used by many industries as an alternative to bisphenol A in the manufacturing of different products such as; polycarbonate plastics, pipe linings and tanks, epoxy resins, cleaning products, baby bottles, thermal papers and food containers (Filik et al., 2019; Freitas et al., 2020). Although BPS has high thermal stability and low biodegradability than bisphenol A, several studies revealed that it also has biological toxicity and estrogenic activity similar to the BPA (Zheng et al., 2016). Furthermore, studies have shown that BPS has high environmental persistence and can also cause chronic diseases such as diabetes, strokes, obesity, and even cancer (Rao et al., 2018). Up to now, different conventional and hyphenated analytical methods have been used to monitor BPS including HPLC-MS (Dualde et al., 2019), UHPLC-MS (Pan et al., 2017), GC-MS (Mandrah et al., 2017), LC-ESI-MS (Yang et al., 2014) and other fluorescence methods (Xiong et al., 2018). Nevertheless, these conventional methods are highly sensitive and accurate but they require expensive and spacious equipment, complex processes, toxic and costly solvents, high energy, and highly professional staff to operate them, hence resulting in expensive and time-consuming analysis (Pang et al., 2020; Hojjati-Najafabadi et al., 2022). The effective alternative to these methods is electrochemical techniques because they offer many advantages over the conventional methods (Mohanraj et al., 2020; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2021; Mehmandoust et al., 2021b) such as; the electrochemical techniques are very fast, much sensitive, easy to operate, inexpensive, eco-friendly and especially there is no need for lengthy sample pretreatment processes and the use of toxic solvents (Alizadeh et al., 2021; Cheraghi et al., 2022; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022d; Mehmandoust et al., 2022). BPS is an electroactive compound due to its phenolic hydroxyl group, which can oxidize to quinone at a certain potential and display an oxidation peak in voltammetric measurements (Wang et al., 2018) but during the oxidation course of BPS, the bare carbon-based electrodes (especially GCEs) usually suffer from the overvoltage which causes the fouling of the electrode and lack of their sensitivity and reproducibility which in turn limits their applicability for routine based sample analysis (Bakhsh et al., 2021; Khand et al., 2021b). Hence, attempts were made to produce different nanomaterials as electrode modifiers to increase the sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility of the electrodes (Gupta et al., 2015; Alavi-Tabari et al., 2018; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2019, 2022c; Hussain et al., 2021, 2022).

Nanostructured metal oxides are the materials that have gained increasing attention from the past few decades in different branch of science (Deeksha et al., 2021; Nishiyama, 2021; Oyeoka et al., 2021; Smaali et al., 2021; Vasseghian et al., 2021; Seid et al., 2022) and especially sensing major and have been proven promising aspirants in this field due to their high surface area, large band gaps, excellent catalytic activity, and tailorable properties (Amin et al., 2021; Buledi et al., 2021a, 2021b; Khand et al., 2021a; Yan et al., 2021; Ghalkhani et al., 2022; Nasehi et al., 2022). Different nanostructured based portable electrochemical sensors have been widely used for the determination of pestcides in the different real samples (Miraki et al., 2019; Alizadeh et al., 2020, 2022; Umapathi et al., 2021, 2022a, 2022b; Taqvi et al., 2022). In metal oxides, SnO2 NPs offer some unique properties like a high degree of transparency in the visible spectrum, low operating temperatures, high stability, and sensitivity, particularly in electrochemical and gas sensing applications (Arya et al., 2018; Buledi et al., 2020; Memon et al., 2020; Panhwar et al., 2021). Tin usually possess two oxidation states Sn+2 and Sn+4 hence it possibly form two type of oxides, i.e. stannous oxide (SnO) and stannic oxide (SnO2) (Tazikeh et al., 2014) SnO2 is more stable than SnO and has a higher bandgap of 3.6 eV than the SnO which has a bandgap between 2.2 and 3.0 eV (Dheyab et al., 2021). SnO2 is an n-type semiconducting material that has a wide profile of potential applications in many fields including lithium-ion batteries (Nowak, 2018), solar cells (Pereira et al., 2017; Smith et al., 2020), optoelectronic devices (Nehru et al., 2012), photocatalysis (Honarmand et al., 2019; Luque et al., 2020), supercapacitor (Velmurugan et al., 2016; Godlaveeti et al., 2021), solid-state gas sensor and especially in electrochemical sensors (Leite et al., 2000; Hsu and Wu, 2019; Yin et al., 2019; Sharma et al., 2020). Due to the high sensitivity, high selective, excellent chemical and thermal stability, low cost, and strong physicochemical interaction with absorbed species, SnO2 NPs have been widely utilized in electrochemical sensing and different environmental monitoring activities (Fadillah et al., 2020; Dheyab et al., 2021; Mohammad et al., 2021). According to the literature, SnO2 NPs can be synthesized by many chemical methods including, the Sol-gel method (Subramaniam et al., 2018), co-precipitation method (Arularasu et al., 2018), microwave-assisted method (Oliveira et al., 2018), combustion route (Vijayan et al., 2020) and hydrothermal method (Sha and Badhulika, 2018; Meng et al., 2019). Among these methods, an easy and effective way to synthesize SnO2 NPs is an aqueous chemical growth method, the advantage of this method is the use of water as a solvent which makes it green, eco-friendly, and very much cost-effective. This method also offers several other advantages over the other methods, like low operational temperature, less processing cost, good homogeneity, and the controlled growth of particles with excellent crystallinity and structural functionalities (Buledi et al., 2021b).

The purpose of this study is to synthesize SnO2 NPs with a facile method and to exploit the novel and reliable resulting material for the electrochemical monitoring of BPS in different water samples. Though, to the best of our knowledge, the modification of electrode's surface with Nafion® blended tin oxide nanoparticles for the electrochemical monitoring of bisphenol-s has not been used till to date. The developed method, based on tin oxide/Nafion/GCE can also be used for the field measurements by exploiting it for onsite detection of BPS analyte. Moreover, the developed sensor has some advantages in the terms of high sensitivity, selectivity and low cost as compare to the other reported methods in the literature.

All chemicals used in this experiment were of analytical grade and used without any additional purifications. Tin chloride salt was purchased from Merck (Sweden), sodium hydroxide, BPS, BPA, KCl, HCl, and TCP were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Double distilled deionized (D.I) water was used throughout the experiment. All glassware was initially washed with tap water then DI water and dried for 30 min at 100 °C before use. 0.1 M solution of all buffers (used in this study) were prepared and their pH was adjusted according to the need by 0.1 M NaOH or HCl solution. 0.1 M stock solution of BPS was prepared in D.I water and diluted according to the need of the experiment.

A simple, prestigious, and low-temperature method “the aqueous chemical growth method” was used for the effective and controlled growth of SnO2 NPs. For this characteristic synthesis, 0.2 M of Tin chloride and 0.4 M of NaOH (1:2 w/w) were accurately measured and added in 400 mL of DI water and stirred for several minutes until the solution was completely homogenized. The beaker containing the solution mixture was properly covered up with aluminum foil and placed in a heating oven for 4 h at 90 °C. Precipitates were formed which were filtered and rinsed properly with DI water to detach the impurities. After washing, the filtered material was again kept in the oven at 100 °C for drying. Finally, the dried material was powdered and filled in a crucible and proceeded to anneal in an electric muffle furnace at 500 °C for 4 h. The resultant SnO2 NPs were stocked in a falcon tube and stored in dark.

To increase the sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and catalytic efficiency of the working electrode (GCE) towards BPS, the surface of the bare electrode was modified with highly fascinating SnO2 NPs. For this typical modification, 20 g of SnO2 NPs were added in a mixture composed of 5 mL of water and 0.1 mL of Nafion® (binder). The prepared solution was sonicated for 20 min to make it homogenize. Before modification of bare GCE surface with SnO2 NPs, the electrode surface was carefully polished with 0.5 μ pore Al2O3 powder and rinsed thoroughly with DI water. The modification was done by a well-known “drop-casting” method in which 8 μL of the prepared SnO2 NPs solution was carefully deposited on the neat and clean surface of the bare electrode. The electrode was then allowed to dry at room temperature.

The vibrational modes and functionalities of SnO2 NPs were examined via FTIR analysis. The FTIR analysis reveals the broad vibrational modes at 3437 cm−1 which denotes (OH) stretching from the atmosphere. The FTIR peak at 1632 cm−1 is a characteristic CO group from tin acetate precursor. The last two peaks at 594 and 625 cm−1 are corresponding to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching of the Sn–O bond. The FTIR peaks of SnO2 NPs are highlighted in Fig. 1 . The crystallinity and the phase purity of prepared nanoparticles were investigated through XRD analysis. The XRD pattern displayed at (110), (101), (200), (211), and (220) with other supported patterns successfully highlight the exceptional crystalline nature of engineered nanoparticles. The size of SnO2 NPs was also examined through the Debye Sherer equation which was observed as 24.9 nm respectively. The XRD patterns of SnO2 NPs are depicted in Fig. 2 . The internal morphology and surface texture of SnO2 NPs were examined via TEM analysis. Fig. 3 (a and b) highlights the TEM images of prepared SnO2 NPs. TEM is an exceptional analytical tool that evaluates the 2D texture and dept analysis of materials. The TEM images disclose the size of SnO2 NPs less than 50 nm. Though the shape of nanoparticles is not seemed to be identical, quite reduced size at the nanoscale can be easily seen from the images. The overall TEM analysis manifested the nanometric size of SnO2 NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of bare and SnO2/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the electrochemical behavior and conductive nature of the bare and SnO2/GCE, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used. In the supporting medium, the bare electrode and SnO2/GCE showed a reduced redox peak current response, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). The SnO2/GCE demonstrated an outstanding conductive nature and less resistive behavior than the bare electrode, resulting in increased redox peak current. While the bare electrode shows less conductive and high resistive behavior than the SnO2/GCE electrode in the supporting medium. Furthermore, bare and SnO2/GCE were directed for EIS measurements to assess the charge transfer kinetics and resistive nature. In EIS analysis, the semicircle curve of the Nyquist plot is monitored, indicating whether the electrodes are resistive or conductive. According to the Nyquist plot, the proposed electrodes will manifest greater charge transfer kinetics if the semicircle curve is narrower. The EIS measurements were carried out in a medium containing [5 mM K3 Fe(CN)6 and K4 Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl respectively. Fig. 4 (b) shows a broader semicircle curve for the bare electrode, which demonstrates lower charge transfer kinetics and higher electron transfer resistance for the bare electrode. In the supporting medium, the SnO2/GCE curve was smaller, demonstrating the excellent conductive nature of SnO2. The entire CV and EIS analysis reveal SnO2 NPs' excellent electrocatalytic activity, implying that the proposed electrode might be used to accurately determine BPS real matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV Ipa response of BPS electrolytes and pH study
ce:section.ce:para: In electrochemistry, the supporting mediums are important and act as an important part of the detection of analytes at various pH levels. The electrolyte that offers a path between the surface of electrochemical sensors and analytes is extremely significant in the electrochemical setup. As a result, selecting a supportive electrolyte for the detection of the targeted analyte is a critical step. Different supporting electrolytes were chosen for the sensitive detection of BPS, such as borate buffer pH of 8, NaOH pH of 12, phosphate buffer (PBS) pH of 6, and Briton Robison buffer (BRB) pH 3. The pH was changed from acidic to basic by adding 0.1 M NaOH and HCl to the specified buffer. The maximum current response of BPS was supervised in PBS supporting electrolyte at an acidic pH of 6 in 70 μM of PBS at a scan sweep of 100 mV/s as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The peak potential of BPS is also affected by the supporting medium having variable pH values. In general, it has been observed that an acidic pH buffer medium pushes the peak potential of an analyte towards a positive potential due to maximum proton transformation. In a basic pH medium, the peak potential of the analyte is shifted towards the negative potential. Though PBS electrolyte manifested the highest Ipa response for BPS in the present experiment and it was selected for the pH study. The pH range of the PBS supporting electrolyte was tested from 3 to 8, with a one-unit difference, ranging from very acidic to basic medium. The current response of BPS increased as the pH increased since the pH experiment began with a strongly acidic medium pH 3. The greatest Ipa response of BPS was observed at pH 6 of the PBS supporting electrolyte. The BPS Ipa responses based on pH are shown in Fig. 5 (b). Consequently, pH 6 of PBS electrolyte was employed in a subsequent investigation of BPS. Moreover, the oxidation phenomenon of BPS was determined by using CV mode. As shown in Fig 5(b), the response of current peak of BPS was steadily enhanced via increasing the pH value, indicating the proptons are involved in the BPS reaction process at the surface of SnO2/GCE. Subsequently, the response of peak current of BPS diminished in higher pH value, which may be attributed to the electrostatic repulsion of BPS anion with negative charge on the surface of SnO2/GCE developed sensor. Therefore, PBS supporting electrolyte with pH value was selected for the sensitive determination of BPS in the aquous media (Rao et al., 2018; Peng et al., 2019). The pH based oxidation mechanism of PBS is shown in Scheme 1 .

The stability is one of the most important parameter in the electrochemical sensors. The fabricated sensor must be stable enough to be used for prolong span of time. For stability measurement the proposed sensor was kept under examination for consecutive two weeks. Each day the response of sensor employed in the analyte was tested and recorded. After two weeks time the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the sensor was calculated which was found less than 5% that highlights the reliability and reusability of SnO2/GCE to be used for prolong time (see Fig. 6 ).

The sensitivity of SnO2/GCE for determining BPS was investigated using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The suggested sensor's linear dynamic range (LDR) was determined by performing a calibration study in DPV mode. In PBS electrolyte pH 6, the LDR was set from 1 to 110 μM at a pulse rate of 70 mV/s. At the different concentrations of BPS, the constructed sensor exhibited an outstanding linear response. The BPS Ipa responses are shown in Fig. 7 (a). To ensure that the Ipa response and peak potential of BPS were maintained, the cycle was repeated (n = 4) at each concentration. The regression results for the BPS Ipa response over a wide range of concentrations are shown in Fig. 7(b). The regression value was calculated to be (R2 = 0.9931) at various concentrations ranging from 1 to 110 μM indicating good linearity of the constructed sensor for BPS. The proposed method's LOD and LOQ for BPS were calculated to be 0.007 μM and 0.031 μM respectively. The LOD of the prepared sensor for BPS is lower than most of the published approaches implying that the SnO2/GCE is extremely sensitive and reliable. The suggested approach is compared to chemically modified electrochemical sensing probes to assess the sensitivity and effectiveness of SnO2/GCE. The developed method is more environmentally friendly, cost-effective, selective, sensitive, and stable than the previously reported method. Table 1 shows a comparison of the proposed approach with previously published electrochemical sensors. Moreover, the sensitivity and the detection limit of proposed method could be effectively increased by employing SnO2 nanoparticles with the carbon based materials especially the graphene and quantom dots. According to the previously reported literature these carbon based materials manifested exceptional conductivity and electrocatalytic properties. Thus, the exploitation of SnO2 nanoparticles with the these materials could flawlessly improve the sensitivity of fabricated nanoparticles. As it has been already stated that the fabricated SnO2 nanoparticles are cost effective and economic than the most the reported literature, thus employing the carbon based materials with SnO2 nanoparticles may increase the cost and preparation steps that would generally affect the reliability of proposed method.

To explore the selective nature of SnO2/GCE, various co-existing metals, such as anion, organic compounds, and cations could be found in real analyte samples. Anions, cations, and organic compounds such as Cl−, Hg+2, K+, Pb+2, HQ, BPA, and TCP were added to the cell along with BPS. The RSD and recovery values of various interferants are shown in Table 2 . Five cycles of each interferant were recorded to calculate the RSD values of interferants. The lowest and highest RSD values were recorded as 2.6 and 4.1% respectively. However, the aggregate RSD values of interferants were less than 5%. This demonstrates SnO2/GCE's good selective tendency towards BPS.

Water samples were collected from two different places of river Indus which were supposed to be contaminated with industrial waste (particularly from Sukkur and Jamshoro, Sindh Pakistan) and brought to the laboratory for investigations. Firstly, the water samples were filtered thrice with Whatman filter paper to separate the dust and other contaminants from the water samples, and then a certain volume of 1:10 water samples was mixed with the PBS electrolyte and subjected to electrochemical measurements. The standard addition method was used to validate the obtained results and 4 replicated measurements were performed. The recovery values for water samples are shown in Table 3 .

In the present work, a simple approach was used to fabricate an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor based on SnO2 nanoparticles for the detection of BPS. To confirm the crystallinity, phase purity, and shape of the engineered nanoparticles, multiple analytical techniques were used, such as FTIR, XRD, and TEM. The SnO2 NPs were deposited on the GCE to make the base electrode more sensitive and conductive. The modified sensor demonstrated an impressive response for the BPS when tested under optimal conditions. The proposed method's linear dynamic range was optimized between 1 and 110 μM. The suggested electrochemical sensor has a lower detection limit than most of the reported methods for the detection of BPS. The developed method's LOD was calculated to be 0.007 respectively. The developed method is shown to be an excellent approach for the determination of BPS. Furthermore, the developed approach is more effective, and cost-efficient than the reported method and could be used commercially.
